sourceDesc.p: Downloaded from https://medium.com/@louBurnard/sensible-eating-the-retour-b7dc25cbb468 and translated from HTML to TEI

div.head: Sensible eating: the retour
div.p: I have just remembered that for about six months starting at the end of August 2012 I used to keep a blog detailing the meals I was eating out during my frequent trips to France and elsewhere, the pretext being ostensibly to reassure other members of my family that I was maintaining at least the semblance of sensible dietary practice. Well, we know how that sort of thing turns out. I found the blog here, and it shows lots of what I think can only be called restaurant porn, and no evidence of calory control whatsoever. So this week, in the interests of disinterested scientific enquiry, I thought it might be instructive to rerun the experience some five years later. Spoiler alert: food porn ahead.

The period covered this time runs from Monday 16th Oct , when I checked into Domitille’s Airbnb flat just down the road from the Ecole Nationale des Chartes in rue Richelieu, to the following weekend, when I proceeded to the Grand Hotel de Tours, for a four night stay finishing today, the 26th. In between the eating I managed to fit in four days of intensive TEI training at the Ecole nationale des Chartes, and three days ditto at the MSH of the Université de Tours. It’s hard work this teaching malarkey : you need plenty of eating to keep it going.

As any fule kno, the ENC is very conveniently located not only for the now beautifully restored Richelieu reading room of the Bibliothèque Nationale, but also for the rue Saint Anne, a narrow little street mostly consisting of the best selection of little Japanese and Korean restaurants in Paris. Which is why every lunch time sees people heading up and down the street to find one that’s got a space for them, and every evening sees people forming hungry queues outside their current favourite. Emmanuel has reasonably reliable views on which establishment to patronise most days.

17To demonstrate our broad-mindedness, however, we started by lunching at a new French establishment called “Kaitleen”, just down rue des petits champs, remarkable for serving lots of “English style” (i.e. crunchy) vegetables, and also for really delicious and freshly made strawberry shortcake. Left to my own devices in the evening, I also decided to stay French. The rognons de veau sautés, followed by a very creditable riz au lait, provided by a rather upscale establishment called Le Saotico, hit the spot at a cost of only €33 with wine.

18 We reverted to tradition for lunch: Jubey being closed for works, we sought out a bowl of noodles and meat at the Kadoya. In the evening, after the post-course beer at le Vaudeville, Emmanuel took me for a walk down towards the Marais, to be more precise to the rue de Vertboiss, where lurks an exceptional French restaurant called “Pramil” after its owner, an ex-academic. Though small and unpretentious in architecture, and modest in menu, this is a table which vaut le detour: top quality cooking at a reasonable price.

figure.head: Filet de St Pierre at Pramil. Cost 51.5

div.p: I had a large amount of fillet de St Pierre (a tasty white fish known in English as John Dory, I learn) associated with a few aubergines. Nothing pretentious as you can see but entirely delicious.

19 Hooray, my favourite Japanese udon bar, Jubey, is open again. They don’t have a website though you’ll find the place listed routinely in those tedious “Top Ten places to eat Noodles in Paris” pages, because they’re too busy cooking noodles. Down the tiny twisty stairs to the little basement we go, gaze in wonder at the varieties on offer. It’s the ideal lunch spot: the service is quick, the food is tasty, there is plenty of scope for choice, within the basic parameters. And it only costs €22, with tea.

In the evening, again left to my own devices, I succumbed to the lure of the duck : at Domaine de Lintillac, a classy duck-themed establishment offering amongst other things, the Choucroute de Canard The Confit de Canard was nicer than the Saucisse de chez nous, which was nicer than the Magret Fumé. The boiled potato and sauerkraut were fine, though not quite what I’d expected.

figure.head: Choucroute de canard avec son saucisson at Domaine de Lintillac. 26.6 euro inc pot de cote.

div.p: 20 I persuaded Emmanuel that we’d like to try Korean for change, so we lunched at the restau Dokkebi in rue des petits champs, which was fine once I’d remembered how to cope with metal chop sticks. And in the evening I went for the celebratory option at a celebrated Parisian bouchon called Aux Lyonnais. The decor is typique, and the food is plausibly authentique. Here I had the salade frisee (naturally enhanced with lardons and a poached egg), the foie de veau, and the pot de figues, all of which were delicious, as was the extremely expensive pichet de Chateauneuf du Pape I’d ordered in a moment of inattention.

figure.head: Happy diners assembling. Note the corks’ nest.
Aux Lyonnais, rue St Marc: 101 euro, of which 40 was the pot de chateauneuf du pape

div.p: 21 My day off. I walked for miles to the Museum of Romantic Life, rue Chaptal in the 9th, formerly home of Georges Sand and currently exhibiting rather more Redoute than one might wish for. But the permanent collection is interesting and in the grounds there is a pleasant tree-filled garden, and a little shed where they serve a decent quiche and salad, which was really all I felt up to consuming to be honest. And in the evening I said my farewell to St Annes street, by dining at the only place without a queue at the door, namely a huge canteen- like establishment called Laï-Laï Ken. This provided a very good seaweed soup, acceptable beef and onions, with tasty rice, followed by a couple of steamed red-bean filled dumplings for that tiny hollow.

figure.head: Diner at Lai Lai Ken, rue St Anne. 29.5 euro including a beer

div.p: 22 Off to Tours. I arrived in time for a late light lunch at the Brasserie l’Univers, where I consumed bavette frites, and a cafe gourmand.

figure.head: Traditional lunch: bavette frites, followed by cafe gourmand. Brasserie l’Univers trots them out for 24.8 euro including a glass of Bourgeuil

div.p: In the evening, as per tradition, I met up with Toshi, Alexei, and Sandrine at La Chope for a quality dinner: salade vertes, St Pierre again, this time just a little one, served on the bone and with all the fiddly bits, including tasty vegetables. And a bowl of fresh red fruit laced with fizzy white wine for pud.

figure.head: La Chope’s take on St Pierre was smaller but just as tasty. Fresh fruit swimming in bubbly white wine.

div.p: 23This year’s lunches at the MSH were held in a large canteen 5 minutes walk away from the MSH car park, overlooking the river Cher, and next door to some local EDF office block. They were no worse than other French canteen lunches and better than some.

In the evening Alexei and I headed for a fairly posh establishment called Le 16 where we dined with the new boss of the CESR and Toshi. Formal dinner conversation in French with an Italian, a Russian, a Japanese, and a Brit seemed a bit odd, but no complaints about the food. I had the velouté de cèpes et marrons, followed by Le cochon torréfié de 7 h au miel et balsamique, purée grand-mère aka slow cooked pork with mash, which was much nicer than it sounds and too filling to permit any dessert.

24th : something called rougaille saucisses appeared on the canteen lunch menu today, but was some distance away from the dish I know under that name, and not just because I eat it with chips. In the evening, Sandrine took me and Toshi out for a much nicer and more relaxed dinner, at a little place called Le Petit Patrimoine, on rue Colbert. I had their take on salad with bits of roast duck, duck pate, and fresh figs, which was both beautiful and delicious, followed by bits of chicken in a rather strange gloopy sauce, which was neither. But the company was good.

figure.head: Duck salad at the Little Heritage: much better than chicken nuggets in peanut sauce.

div.p: 25Alas, my last day in Tours. For lunch there was a quite respectable daube de boeuf, with the usual frites, and some rather less usual baked fennel.

figure.head: Quite a decent canteen lunch: note the baked fennel

div.p: For dinner, left to my own resources, after a long walk, I decided to revisit the Brasserie du chien jaune just over the road from the station and still as good as I said it was back in 2012: it’s been through at least two changes of proprietor since then, but the 1920s décor is just the same, and the cooking is more ambitious. Definitely worth a visit. Here’s the slate :

figure.head: Menu “magret” at the Yellow Dog. I had each of the second alternatives. See below. Total including wine 38.9 and worth every centime.
Duck salad (this time with foie gras as well as bits of duck), gigot et grenailles (excellent), vacherin (superb mixture of meringue, ice cream, fresh fruit, nuts, caramel syrup, and mint).

div.p: So, what have we learned? Firstly that the price of a meal is not entirely reliable as an indication of its quality. Secondly that the art of the French restauranteur is not dead, even in the age of macdo and le snacking. And third, that I really like eating.
